retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Important Elements of Image Acquisition - Screen film X-ray images are produced when an image receptor cassette is exposed to an existing x-ray beam. [10] The image receptor cassette contains a radiographic film between 2 intensifying screens. The radiographic film is made up of a base covered by film emulsion. The film emulsion component contains silver bromide crystals, which are light-sensitive. Silver bromide crystals interact with light photons to form a radiographic image. The technologist plays an important role in manipulating kVp and mAs in image production. These factors influence the quantity and quality of x-rays, influencing contrast and image quality. The source-image distance (SID) is the distance from the tube to the receptor. Along with mAs and kVp, the SID influences factors such as contrast, distortion, magnification, and optical density. Functional technique charts (including fixed-kVp and high-kVp charts) are used to produce radiographs depending on the type of study (body part) and other factors, such as the x-ray machine itself. Anatomically Programmed Radiography plays a role in automatically selecting kVp and mAs depending on the technologist’s selection of an anatomic part. Screen film radiography has been nearly completely replaced by digital radiography in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In summary, we discussed important components of radiographic image quality and various factors affecting image quality. This knowledge is useful for obtaining high-quality digital radiographs with the lowest possible radiation dose to improve the clinician's diagnostic accuracy.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology, Image Production and Evaluation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are several things to consider when evaluating an image. The contrast of an image gets produced by the difference in tissue density displayed on a radiograph, and it can thus be manipulated to distinguish an area of interest. Contrast is dependent on the film density, film processing, curve of the film, and the use of intensifying screens. The density is the amount of light transmitted through the film and measures the degree of film darkening. Distortion of the image can depend on the size, shape, and position of the target structure within the body. Identification markers serve to differentiate the location, type of image, position, and orientation of the image. These markers are necessary to identify and analyze the anatomical structures correctly. Awareness of these factors is essential when evaluating radiographic images. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Image Quality - Several factors determine image quality but are best characterized by a high signal-to-noise ratio (low noise) and good spatial resolution. Additional factors include factors relating to film processing, factors relating to geometric characteristics (such as magnification), and factors relating to the patient. The technologist has an important role in maintaining good image quality. For example, preventing patient movement helps reduce motion blur. [3] X-rays can either pass through a patient or are scattered by Compton interaction, which results in noise. Also, high kVp worsens the signal-to-noise ratio, while the thickness of the tissue being penetrated also increases noise. Using higher kVp results in an x-ray beam of higher average energy requiring a shorter exposure time and mA (together referred to as tube current exposure time product value or mAs), increasing patient penetration but also resulting in increased scatter and decreased image contrast. The converse is also true. A lower kVp setting requires increased mAs, resulting in decreased patient penetration, decreased scatter, and increased contrast. Carefully balancing these factors is essential for managing the relationship between patient dose, image contrast, and quality. Reduction of scatter radiation can be achieved by using grids, which have the added benefit of improving contrast and play an important role in producing an adequate quality and interpretable radiographic image. Although kVp increases noise, it also reduces patient dose. Collimators are devices that restrict/narrow beams and control scatters, resulting in increased signal-to-noise and decreased patient dose. In addition to image contrast, spatial resolution is an important measure of image quality. A smaller field of view results in a higher spatial resolution of the image and a decreased overall dose to the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Digital Radiography - The computed radiographic image receptor, an elementary form of a digital detector, is similar yet different from screen-film imaging in a few ways. One similarity is their mutual use of X-ray-sensitive plates. Computed radiography using storage phosphors uses trapped electrons in metastable states to produce an image instead of a scintillator used in screen-film radiography. Metastable electrons are released with a stimulating laser beam, with the emission of light on the return of these electrons to a ground state. [11] This light emission is used to establish an image. Computed radiography also comparatively has a reduced radiation dose and better contrast resolution when compared to screen radiography (although the resolution is worse in digital radiography). Flat Panel Detectors are classically associated with digital detectors. It is faster than film radiography and includes direct and indirect systems. Explained simply, indirect systems require a latent image to be released as a light, then converted to a charge and a digital image. In contrast, direct systems directly convert x-rays into charge (and subsequently an image) without this intermediate step. Indirect systems employ a cesium iodide scintillator, which constitutes most flat-panel detectors, while direct systems employ amorphous selenium.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Tissue-specific absorption and attenuation of X-ray photons with resultant variable loss of photon energy and subsequent detection by the image receptor produces the variable densities seen on a radiograph. X-ray energy traveling through the body typically results in one of 3 outcomes: absorption, transmission, and scatter. In general, more dense materials (eg, bone) absorb more energy (X-ray photons) than that less dense materials (eg, fat) The patient's tissues absorb approximately two-thirds of the X-ray photon energy produced by X-ray tubes. About one-third results in scatter radiation and less than 1% is transmitted to the image cassette/receptor to create a radiologic image.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- Distortion
retrieved_docs.content: Beam profiles and paths of the photons also influence the quality and characteristics of an image. X-ray divergence patterns can be described by photons directed linearly toward the center, whereas those on the periphery tend to splay out more radially. [8] As a result, the anatomy located on the periphery of the beam profile and lateral to the center suffers some degree of distortion. However, some commonly manipulatable factors can limit the distortion in an image: centering, source image receptor distance (SID), and object image receptor distance (OID). Centering refers to positioning the anatomical portion of interest in line with the central point of the X-ray. SID is the distance between the X-ray tube and the image receptor and is inversely proportional to magnification/distortion. In other words, the greater the SID, the less magnification or distortion is apparent in the image. The standard SID (ref) utilized is set to be 100 cm. OID is the distance between the object (eg, femur, abdomen) and the image receptor, directly proportional to magnification. The greater the OID, the greater the magnification. Considering all 3 factors, the most optimal positioning for X-ray imaging would be to have the anatomy of interest in the center of the X-ray beam, the beam sufficiently distanced from the image receptor, and the image receptor as close as possible to the anatomy being imaged. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Controlling Factors of X-Ray Production
retrieved_docs.content: X-ray production control factors that X-ray technicians manipulate to produce technically adequate images include the number of photons produced by an X-ray beam, measured in mAs, and the energy of the photons produced, measured in peak kilovoltage (kVp). The mAs reflect the image quantity/intensity, and the kVp reflects the image quality. The combinations may vary depending on the patient's clinical scenario being imaged and the radiographer's experience taking the image. These concepts are important for radiographers to produce technically adequate radiographs. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Production Procedures -- Issues of Concern -- Grid
retrieved_docs.content: Scatter reduction is primarily addressed with the use of grids. A grid between the patient and the receptor comprises X-ray-absorbing material (eg, lead) interspaced with low-attenuating material (eg, carbon fiber). [11] The amount of scatter reduction a grid provides is directly proportional to the ratio between the height of the grid and the interspacing, also known as the grid ratio. The greater the grid ratio (eg, 10:1, 12:1), the greater the scatter reduction, increasing image contrast and patient exposure dose. The ratio of the increase in image contrast and the patient dose is referred to as the contrast improvement factor and the Bucky factor, respectively. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Effective dose refers to the amount of radiation received by the whole body, and measurement is in millisievert (mSv). Generally speaking, various procedures entail different effective radiation doses based on site and use of contrast. For example, a radiograph of the spine has an approximate effective dose of around 1 mSv. [10] A radiograph of the extremity ranges within the upper limits of normal between 0.17 to 2.7 microSv. [10] To better place these doses in context, we can compare these exposures with natural radiation we obtain from our surroundings, which usually approximates to 3 mSv per year. [11] A spine X-ray, therefore, is comparable to the natural background radiation exposure for six months. An extremity radiograph compares to natural background radiation exposure of 3 hours. Bone densitometry and mammography studies have an approximate effective dose of around 0.001 mSv and 0.4 mSv, respectively, comparable to 3 hours and six weeks of background radiation, respectively. [12] Radiography, therefore, in the setting of cumulative exposure is not without risks in patients who require frequent imaging studies. An X-ray technician plays an instrumental role in the acquisition of interpretable and high-quality X-ray images. A working and constant relationship between a radiologist and an X-ray technician is essential in troubleshooting and acquiring images in the appropriate diagnosis of a patient. X-ray technicians are also critical in preventing artifacts, taking brief medical histories, ensuring appropriate laterality, appropriate positioning, and adjusting and maintaining various equipment involved in X-ray acquisition.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: X-ray radiation production is the first step of a multistep process that creates a radiograph. The process involves generating an X-ray beam, passing X-ray photons through a patient's biological tissues, and interacting exiting photons with an image receptor to generate a film-screen or digital system radiograph (see Image. Simplified Schematic View of an X-ray Generator). It is important to understand the general physical principles of X-ray production. Additionally, a general understanding of factors affecting X-ray attenuation, such as the absorption characteristics of anatomic tissues, is crucial to understanding how technically adequate radiographs are produced and artifact production is limited. Lastly, understanding technically adequate anatomic features of a radiograph can be used to troubleshoot discrepancies in the production process.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Issues of Concern -- Acquisition Geometry
retrieved_docs.content: Image acquisition geometric factors affecting image quality include source-to-image receptor distance, orientation, magnification amount, and focal spot size. Changes in source-to-image receptor distance result in variations in relative magnifications of anatomic structures in the image.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction -- Contrast
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic contrast is a fractional difference in the signal or brightness between the structure of interest and its surroundings. [3] Contrast is generated by differential attenuation of X-rays by different tissues. Radiographic contrast is proportional to the atomic number, density, and tissue thickness. For example, X-ray attenuation is least in the air and higher in bone and between soft tissues. In digital radiography, the contrast can be adjusted using image post-processing techniques where pixel values are changed to provide the expected contrast range depending on specific clinical requirements. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal is towards the body’s center.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Distal denotes being away from the body’s center.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Medial indicates towards the midline.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1292
retrieved_docs.content: The Bone Mineral Density Test is classified as part of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, a category that also encompasses Other x‑ray and Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen as its sub‑modalities. This classification indicates that bone mineral density testing is considered a form of diagnostic radiology alongside other x‑ray procedures, including generic other x‑ray examinations and soft tissue abdominal x‑ray imaging, thereby grouping these imaging techniques together for organized clinical use and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 111
retrieved_docs.content: and all imaging procedures are linked to a standardized diagnostic/staging answer set (PhenX), supporting their use in patient assessment and research phenotyping. This integrated hierarchy enables clinicians to select appropriate modalities, understand methodological bases, and navigate relationships among bone density testing, plain radiography, and CT across chest, abdomen, pelvis, retroperitoneum, genitourinary, and central nervous system for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1658
retrieved_docs.content: Bone Mineral Density Test is classified within the category Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. CT follow-up is an instance of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques and serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action. The broader group Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques includes procedures such as Soft tissue X-ray of abdomen and is itself regarded as a type of Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). Computed tomography imaging - action, which underlies CT follow-up, is also a method of the same Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). Together, these relationships illustrate how specific imaging examinations like Bone Mineral Density Test and CT follow-up fit into a hierarchical framework of diagnostic radiology techniques, linking them to both standard and unlisted computed tomography procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 288
retrieved_docs.content: The Bone Mineral Density Test is classified as part of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. CT follow-up is an instance of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques and functions as a method of Computed tomography imaging – action, which in turn is a method of an Unlisted computed tomography procedure (e.g., diagnostic, interventional). Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques also serves as a subclass of the Unlisted computed tomography procedure (e.g., diagnostic, interventional) and includes child modalities such as Other x-ray and Soft tissue X-ray of abdomen. Together, these relationships illustrate the hierarchical organization of radiologic procedures, linking specific tests like the Bone Mineral Density Test and CT follow‑up examinations to broader categories and unlisted CT procedures, and highlighting the sub‑modalities of x‑ray imaging within the same diagnostic radiology framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest X-ray functions as a central reference point that links both advanced imaging studies and clinical encounters. A CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis is primarily mapped from the routine chest X‑ray, indicating that the plain‑film examination often guides the decision to obtain this comprehensive computed tomography scan. In a similar way, a Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium is listed as another mapping derived from the routine chest X‑ray, reflecting its role in extending diagnostic evaluation beyond the initial radiograph. Clinically, an Encounter due to routine chest X‑ray is directly associated with the routine chest X‑ray itself, establishing a specific relationship between the patient visit and the imaging order. This encounter is also categorized as an Unspecified examination with a broader relationship, suggesting that while the primary focus is the chest X‑ray, the encounter may encompass additional, less defined assessments. Together, these connections illustrate how a routine chest X‑ray serves as a pivotal trigger for more detailed CT imaging and as a focal element of patient encounters within the healthcare workflow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2008
retrieved_docs.content: Bone Mineral Density Test is categorized within the broader group of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, as is the modality labeled Other chest x‑ray. The category Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques serves as a parent class that encompasses several specific imaging procedures, including Other x‑ray and Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen, each identified as child sub‑categories of this group. In addition, the Other chest x‑ray procedure is positioned under the parent imaging study known in Arabic as التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر (computed tomography of the chest), indicating that it is considered a sub‑type within the chest CT imaging hierarchy. Together, these relationships define a structured taxonomy of radiologic examinations, linking individual tests such as bone mineral density assessment and various chest and abdominal X‑ray techniques to their overarching diagnostic radiology classifications and to more specific parent–child imaging categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: how a simple chest X‑ray can serve as the gateway to a hierarchy of imaging techniques—from plain radiography to advanced CT of multiple body regions—while also connecting to specific disease associations and broader neuro‑imaging frameworks essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: The Bone Mineral Density Test is classified as part of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, a broad radiologic category that also encompasses the Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen as one of its child procedures. This grouping indicates that both bone density assessment and abdominal soft‑tissue imaging are organized under the same diagnostic radiology specialty, reflecting their related methodological framework and relevance in clinical imaging workflows.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1962
retrieved_docs.content: Bone Mineral Density Test is categorized under the broader group of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, which also encompasses imaging modalities such as Other x‑ray and Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen. Within this same diagnostic radiology domain, pelvimetry is recognized as a specific type of examination, and CT pelvimetry is identified as the primary imaging method mapped to pelvimetry. Together, these procedures illustrate how bone density assessment, abdominal soft‑tissue imaging, and pelvic measurement studies are interconnected components of the wider field of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, each serving distinct but complementary roles in patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2199
retrieved_docs.content: "Other chest x-ray" is classified as a subcategory within the broader imaging modality of chest computed tomography (التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر), indicating that this specific type of chest X‑ray is considered a child or specialized form of chest CT imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: as a child modality, indicating that tomography (including CT) is considered a specialized subcategory within the spectrum of plain radiographic techniques. This network of hierarchical, mapping, and metadata relationships integrates various CT examinations—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced, single‑region and multi‑region—under unified anatomical and procedural categories, supporting consistent terminology and facilitating comprehensive patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that can be expanded into more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a CT of the chest and abdomen and a CT of the chest are directly mapped, indicating a direct and principal relationship between the plain radiograph and these focused CT studies. Additionally, the routine chest x-ray is linked as an alternative or supplementary reference for broader CT examinations, including a computerized tomography of the brain and chest as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen. These mappings illustrate how a basic chest x-ray can inform and guide the selection of more comprehensive CT imaging of multiple thoracic and cranial regions, supporting nuanced diagnostic decision‑making and patient care.
